responseSet.title: Archaic Introjects and the Cosmology of H. G. Wells
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Archaic Introjects and the Cosmology of H. G. Wells

Alan Parkin M.D.

Alan Parkin

Alan Parkin, M.D.
276 St. Clair Avenue West
Toronto, Ontario
Canada M4V 1R9

Alan Parkin  

SUMMARY

The roles of the archaic loving and hating introjects are traced in the early scientific romances and the life work of H. G. Wells.

The preambivalent polarization of the early loving introjects of an archaic ego ideal (giving rise to utopian fantasies and, later, to promulgations of a new world state) and the early hostile introjects of an archaic superego (giving rise to fears of death and, later, to fears of cosmic dissolution) is represented in eschatological preoccupations with death, the Last Judgment, heaven and hell. These religious preoccupations are derivatives of wishes for maternal union and bliss on the one hand, and of castration anxiety and fears of personal annihilation on the other.

Further transformations of the archaic introjects are traced through an identification with the role of redeemer, and later, through his scientific studies, to an espousal of T. H. Huxley's teachings of organic evolution and to the development of cosmological themes in his work.

[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

THE CULTURE OF NARCISSISM. By Christopher Lasch. New York: Norton, 1978, 268 pp., $11.95.

It has become commonplace to label the culture of the seventies "narcissistic." Narcissism replaced "alienation" as the fashionable label for the people, trends, and events of the decade. No other word captured its bewildering diversity; the breakdown of the family, schools that fail to teach, stagflation in the economy, and the proliferation of self-indulgent "life styles." People seemed absorbed in themselves and uninterested in the causes that gripped the feverish sixties. The present infatuation with the word, however, might seem somewhat surprising. At a time when major news magazines publish a new refutation of Freud every week, the same publications adopt, usually without crediting him, a concept he adumbrated well over sixty years ago. Certainly Freud would be no happier with the heavy-handed moralizing associated with much of the current writing about narcissism than he was with the earlier propaganda for sexual liberation which so many read into his work. Nor would he have liked the way narcissism is used loosely to mean selfishness as once alienation was equated with disaffection. It is to his credit that Christopher Lasch, author of the surprisingly popular The Culture of Narcissism, recognizes the danger inherent in appropriating technical terms for polemical purposes: "Unless we are content to moralize under the cover of psychiatric jargon…we need to use [narcissism] more rigorously than it is used in popular social criticism and with an awareness of its clinical implications." While applauding the sentiment, we wonder how well Lasch has followed his own advice.

Lasch, an eminent historian at the University of Rochester, has combined intellectual history and social criticism before. In Haven in a Heartless World, psychoanalytic concepts are central to his critical analysis. He faults social scientists for presenting a falsely optimistic picture of the modern family. Recognizing the family's progressive loss of economic, social, and political functions, they nevertheless sought to justify the internal strains these losses caused by, paradoxically, attacking the nuclear family as too rigid and authoritarian.

Wayne Myers M.D.

Wayne Myers

Wayne A. Myers, M.D.
1385 York Avenue
New York, New York 10021

Wayne Myers  

SUMMARY

Material is presented from two hypochondriacal patients who insisted on using their own pillows on top of the bolster-pillow

484

on the analyst's couch. A number of important transference fantasies are seen as underlying this unusual resistance to the progress of the analysis. In addition, the analyst's countertransference feelings are described.

[[RunningHead]]

Sexual Excitement

By Robert J. Stoller. New York: Pantheon, 1979, 281 pp., $11.95.

Vann Spruiell M.D.

Vann Spruiell

Vann Spruiell, M.D.
New Orleans, La.

Vann Spruiell  

The expectant psychoanalytic reviewer might be forgiven some complacency as he began a book about sexual excitement by Robert Stoller. He could expect writing that was refreshing and insouciant. And he would know that Stoller had already made substantial contributions to the psychoanalytic theory of sexuality. The foundations of that theory were laid when Freud (1905) dared to uncover the multiple infantile roots and the unconscious dimensions of adult sexuality. Later (1912), Freud commented on the immaturity, psychoneurotic distortions, and infantilisms of the sexual lives of "civilized people." After the dual instinct theory became a cornerstone of mainstream thinking, it was easier to recognize the clinical fact that derivatives of aggression, more or less tamed, are present in all sexual acts, fantasies, and feelings. In that tradition, one knew beforehand that Stoller would argue that for all people some of the time, and most people all of the time, the experience of sexual excitement is less than mature, contains infantilisms, and retains elements of covert or even overt destructiveness.

But for this reviewer the task turned out to be difficult. He could recall no psychoanalytic book that caught him up in such an intense fantasied dialogue with the author. He found himself enthusiastically agreeing, disagreeing, arguing; he was sometimes delighted, sometimes irritated; at some moments he wished there were a way to literally applaud and at others to literally decry. Sometimes all he felt was a mild sadness.

Sexual Excitement is not so much about sexual excitement in general as it is an exploration of the unconscious roots and genetic origins of a complex masturbation fantasy in a young woman. It is not so much about her unconscious fantasies as it is a remarkable attempt to truly portray a specific analytic process. It is not so much an attempt to portray the process as to interpret it in certain controversial ways. It is not so much an attempt to interpret as to grapple with certain philosophical questions concerning the nature of "self," of intentionality, of responsibility.

[[RunningHead]]

The Technique of Child Psychoanalysis. Discussions with Anna Freud

By Joseph Sandler, Hansi Kennedy, and Robert L. Tyson. Cambridge, Mass.: Harvard University Press, 1980, 277 pp., $17.50.

Jules Glenn M.D.

Jules Glenn

Jules Glenn, M.D.
New York City

Jules Glenn  

"This book describes the essential features of the treatment situation in one particular milieu, the Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic in London" (p. 1), as the authors modestly put it. In addition, it serves as a model for the child-analytic technique most analysts in the United States and many analysts in other countries adhere to, and which Anna Freud was instrumental in developing. This model, in accordance with the authors' high standards, encompasses ideals that are very difficult to attain. The technique described differs in theory and practice from the Kleinian approach and from other psychotherapeutic methods that derive from analytic principles.

The book emerged from a research project to determine the nature of child analysis at the Hampstead Clinic. After years of work, during which Miss Freud and her associates gathered information from therapists at this clinic and arranged the data in an extensive index, Sandler organized a weekly discussion group. The participants, who included Anna Freud and the authors as well as others, examined the indexed material in detail. The Technique of Child Psychoanalysis incorporates the substance of these discussions, which were taped. Because of their special interest and importance, many of Anna Freud's comments appear verbatim. The contributions of the other discussants blend into the text.

The result is a most thorough discussion of many basic issues of psychoanalytic theory and practice. It is not truly a textbook, for it assumes the reader has mastered the basic tenets of child analysis. It is not a handbook, not a "cookbook" volume with specific directions, but a careful examination of principles and observations. Because the discussions were based on clinical data, the reader will be gratified to find the theoretical conclusions are clarified by actual instances and the clinical data enriched by profound theoretical explorations.

The book is divided into sections on: The Framework of Treatment; the Therapeutic Relationship; the Child's Modes of Expression; Interpretation and Intervention; and the Outcome of Treatment. All sections are quite inclusive. For instance, the 11 chapters of the section on interpretation and intervention contain chapters on clarification and confrontation, significant interpretations, selection and timing, working through, physical contact and gratification, as well as termination.

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child, Vol. 35

Edited by Albert J. Solnit, Ruth S. Eissler, Anna Freud, Phyllis Greenacre, Marianne Kris, Peter B. Neubauer. New Haven: Yale University Press, 1980, 465 pp., $35.00.

Isidor Bernstein M.D.

Isidor Bernstein

Isidor Bernstein, M.D.
Great Neck, New York

Isidor Bernstein  

This volume maintains the format of its predecessors by division into sections. The first section is devoted to problems of development. E. James Anthony, in his usual lively style, discusses "The Family and the Psychoanalytic Process in Children." He demonstrates the impact of the family on the analytic process in the case of a nine-year-old girl suffering from fears of dying. The severe psychopathology of the parents influenced the child's construction of an internalized ideal family which was reflected in the transference.

The increased psychoanalytic interest in narcissism and the self is evidenced by the inclusion of three related articles. Beulah Kramer Amsterdam and Morton Levitt distinguish self-awareness from the sense of self. They credit several factors in the second year of life with aiding the development of self-other differentiation and self-awareness. Gerald Stechler and Samuel Kaplan report on developmental research regarding the self. It is based on longitudinal studies of well babies begun in 1949 at the James J. Putnam Children's Center. An example is given of a child studied from birth to one year of age. The third article is by Paul M. Brinich, who discusses the wide range of effects of adoption on the child's self- and object representations.

Also in this section is a brief historical survey of the use and disuse of reconstruction by Phyllis Greenacre, who is encouraged by the reawakened interest in it. Sebastiano Santostefano looks at cognition from the standpoint of an analyst. He sees the "new" look at cognition as a "viable alternative to Piaget's model" and proposes his own model. Beth A. Bernstein draws attention to the reactions of siblings of twins. Such siblings often feel closed out of the twin relation, an intensification of the feeling of exclusion from the parental dyad. The children she describes developed intense sibling rivalry and searched for twin surrogates for themselves. The closing article in this section is by S. Louis Mogul who sees anorexia nervosa as a pathological variant of normal adolescent asceticism.

[[RunningHead]]

Metaphors and Isomorphisms: Analogical Reasoning in Beyond the Pleasure Principle

Cecilio Paniagua M.D.

Cecilio Paniagua

Cecilio Paniagua, M.D.
3000 Connecticut Ave., N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20008

Cecilio Paniagua  

SUMMARY

The mind uses analogies in order to understand the unknown. The utilization of these is explored in Freud's writings and in science in general.

Analogies can be metaphorical (figurative) or isomorphic (literal); the latter are used in the induction of scientific hypotheses.

The distinction between these two forms of comparing can be ambiguous. The inappropriate transformation of metaphors into isomorphisms in the construction of theory leads to illegitimate inferences. This paper comments on the use of this type of vitiated logic in Beyond the Pleasure Principle, particularly with reference to Freud's comparison of protozoan biology with human psychology.

[[RunningHead]]

The Concordance to the Standard Edition of the Complete Psychological Works of Sigmund Freud

Edited by Samuel A. Guttman, Randall L. Jones, and Stephen M. Parrish. Boston: G. K. Hall, 1980, 6 Vols., lxxiii + 5512 pp., $525.00.

Stanley Goodman M.D.

Stanley Goodman

Stanley Goodman, M.D.
Berkeley, California

Stanley Goodman  

All English-speaking psychoanalysts are indebted to the editors for their inspired conception and dedicated labor in producing this important and facilitating resource for scholarly work in psychoanalytic research, editing, or education. A note of awed respect is also certainly due Cornell University's IBM 370/168 computer which was crucial to the practical realization of this monumental task. All Freud's words in context, as translated by James Strachey, with the omissions to be noted below, are made relatively easily accessible. Study of his word usage, imagery, verification and location of phrases (including the tracing of exact and similar repetitions), clues to the chronology of concepts, and other aspects of the written cannon are waiting to be revealed. The interested investigator must possess, however, sufficient analytic sophistication as well as ingenuity to ask the genii questions that will fully exploit its potential. It is obvious that similar requirements exist for the most effective use of every powerful resource. None of them, certainly including all computers, reveal their greatest realizable capacity to the unskilled or the beginner; they do not much inform the inadequately informed or respond to a simple "Open, Sesame!" And so it is with the computer-generated Concordance, which is certainly capable of responding to trivial questions with trivial answers; nevertheless, I believe it also demonstrates promise of responding to imaginative and prepared scholars with valuable additional insights into the unique heritage contained in Freud's writings.

A brief description of the Concordance's design may be helpful at this point, particularly for readers who are not familiar with what is primarily a word index as distinguished from the more familiar subject index. The two forms are significantly supplementary; they are not, and are not intended to be, substitutive in function.

[[RunningHead]]

The Adolescent Passage: Developmental Issues

By Peter Blos. New York: International Universities Press, 1979, 521 pp., $30.00.

J. Burland M.D.

J. Burland

J. Alexis Burland, M.D.
Bala-Cynwyd, Pennsylvania

J. Burland  

It is good to have Peter Blos's major articles concerning adolescence collected in one volume. His contribution to the psychoanalytic understanding of this developmental phase is, of course, unique; he has claimed the territory, so to speak, and these articles make clear how justified is his claim.

Collections of papers do have their problems, and this one is no exception. An attempt is made to give the book a narrative line with the articles grouped by topic, and with introductions to sections appended to fit each group into an overall structure; but as is usually the case, this attempt is not really successful. To read the book from start to finish is to experience much repetitiousness, especially when Blos summarizes his contributions in article after article. One leaps about in time, also, as the articles, not chronologically arranged, cover some thirty-two years, from 1946 through 1978; as Blos's concepts, not to mention psychoanalytic theory in general, have continued to evolve over these years, at times one feels pushed forward and backward in the flow of the history of our science. Further, the articles were written for a variety of audiences of varying degrees of sophistication, from prestigious lectureships before knowledgeable audiences through psychoanalytic presentations and publications to general or child-psychiatric ones. And finally, the focus of the papers varies from complex theoretical formulations to clinical presentations to speculations on sociocultural phenomena.

The unifying thread is Blos's mind, however, and that is what makes the book so valuable. His is a fascinating mind to get to know. Wisdom is the word that best categorizes its most striking quality, that and a mature and strong sense of irony. For instance, in Chapter I, "The Generation Gap: Fact or Fiction," and in Chapter II, "Reflections on Modern Youth: Aggression Reconsidered," he demonstrates clearly how it can be psychological insights into the complexities of the mind that best illuminate the hidden dimensions and overdetermined structure of seemingly monolithic and simplistic cultural, social, and political idealisms. Unlike many of his colleagues, Blos takes none of the all-too-often reiterated cliches about adolescents and adolescence at face value.

[[RunningHead]]

The Alternate Schools and the Self

Ernst Ticho Ph.D.

Ernst Ticho

Ernst A. Ticho, Ph.D.
3120 Brandywine Street, N. W.
Washington, D. C. 20008

Ernst Ticho  

SUMMARY

While Adler, Jung, Horney, and Sullivan varied in their definitions of the self, concepts of the self played a central role in

861

their theories. The alternate schools all refer to the subjective, creative, experiencing aspects of the psyche in their concepts of self. The thinking of scientists is intimately connected with their personality. The shape their theories take often can be shown to have connections with their early life. An attempt is made to illuminate the contributions made to Jung's theories by the circumstances of his early life.

[[RunningHead]]

Man's Reach

Rebecca Solomon M.D.

Rebecca Solomon

Rebecca Z. Solomon, M.D.
85 Jefferson Street
Hartford, Connecticut 06106

Rebecca Solomon  

DURING A DISCUSSION OF THE EGO IDEAL OF the psychoanalyst, at the 1977 International Psychoanalytical Congress in Jerusalem, a speaker quoted Robert Browning's lines, "A man's reach should exceed his grasp or what's a Heaven for." I thought he had misquoted, for I remembered the lines as a statement—a man's reach exceeds his grasp—not as an injunction—a man's reach should exceed his grasp. When I suggested to the speaker that I thought he had erred, he quickly agreed with me. I was uneasy about my memory, and when I returned home I reread the poem. I was wrong. Browning had written: "A man's reach should exceed…" I pondered my error and my friend's quick concurrence. It occurred to me that our experience as analysts accounted for our changing the text. As analysts we daily hear accounts of the unhappy consequences of excessive ambition. We know that man's reach does indeed exceed his grasp. The pursuit of Heaven—however it may be defined—is ubiquitous. While aspiration can foster gratifying accomplishment, it can also engender disappointment, bitterness, and psychological disability.

Browning's (1855) poem, "Andrea del Sarto," from which the quotation is taken, poignantly illustrates a man's struggle to reconcile his ambitions and ideals and his attempt to assuage the narcissistic wound that results from failure to achieve his goals. The poem is a monologue by Andrea del Sarto, known as the Faultless Painter.

Presidential Address, plenary presentation, Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1980.

SUMMARY

Two clinical excerpts illustrate the patient's use of telephoning as an equivalent for and a symbol of masturbation. Some of the many and multidetermined latent meanings of telephoning are indicated.

responseSet.title: The Current Psychoanalytic Dialogue: Its Counterpart in Renaissance Philosophy
responseSet.sourceTitle: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Current Psychoanalytic Dialogue: Its Counterpart in Renaissance Philosophy

Simon Grolnick M.D.

Simon Grolnick

Simon A. Grolnick, M.D.
North Shore University Hospital
300 Community Drive
Manhasset, New York 11030

Simon Grolnick  

SUMMARY

It is my premise that psychoanalysis is involved in a phase of metapsychological and technical debate typical during the course of intellectual history. In general, the more traditional school textually conceptualizes psychoanalysis as a natural, observational science involving subject-object duality, determinism, and causality, and tends to separate theory and practice, interspersing a force-energy metapsychology. The recent critics

697

of traditional theory view psychoanalysis as closer to cultural science and art. They do not subscribe to a strict determinism and conceptualize that they work more within a field of free will. Their tendency to keep theory close to practice leads to more experience-colored, closer to the clinical, theoretical models. I have tried to provide some early evidence that, through mutual influence, a dialectic is rising above the heat of this debate, with each group incorporating some of the ideas of the other.

At the same time, since all differences are not true polarities, and not all polarities can be reconciled, the possibility that psychoanalytic theory will necessarily reach a new level of synthesis must be looked upon with caution. It is hoped that a broad view of this theoretical struggle in psychoanalysis can be enriched by an awareness among its participants of the related counterparts in Western intellectual history.

[[RunningHead]]

The Scientific Credibility of Freud's Theories and Therapy

By Seymour Fisher and Roger Greenberg. New York: Basic Books, 1977, 502 pp., $22.50.

Frederick Shevin M.D.

Frederick Shevin

Frederick F. Shevin, M.D.
Birmingham, Michigan

Frederick Shevin  

Validation of psychoanalytic theory by interdisciplinary studies is an acceptable exercise. Unfortunately, this book does not succeed in its purpose. The authors are academic psychologists who claim that Freudian theory has never been adequately tested along interdisciplinary lines and is to be criticized for maintaining Freud's position that the actual conduct of a psychoanalysis is the only way its theory can be tested. They regard this approach as fallacious because of the built-in errors of the analytic situation (countertransference distortion, the analyst's personal stake in understanding the patient's statements as fitting his theoretical expectations and biases, and inaccuracies in reproducing the content of sessions). They feel that the psychoanalytic establishment has not recognized its scientific weaknesses and has encouraged dogmatism by avoidance of proper validation studies.

Whatever changes have occurred have not been the result of scientific weeding but of political shifts in the psychoanalytic power structure. Therefore it would appear to them that the death instinct theory has been officially deleted from party dogma.

While it is necessary to subject psychoanalytic theory to scientific evaluation and criticism, the approach of Robert Holt, in his introduction to the Freud abstracts, is preferred. He sees Freud's theoretical formulations as partial truths, set down to serve a purpose and constantly subject to change as time and experience provide new data or better perspectives for drawing conclusions. The body of knowledge so accumulated is a historical documentation carrying within it the seeds of change.

Setting this aside, the authors make a commendable effort to validate theory by what appears to be an exhaustive search of the literature. There are approximately 2,000 references. Certain Freudian paradigms are reduced to testable hypotheses and the literature (almost exclusively psychological) is reviewed for validation studies. The effort seems a worthwhile one, but the authors are confronted by insurmountable methodological problems. They recognize these flaws but proceed despite them.

[[RunningHead]]

Mothers, Fathers, and Children: Explorations in the Formation of Character in the First Seven Years

By Sylvia Brody and Sidney Axelrad, in association with Ethel Horn, Marsha Moroh, and Marvin Taylor. New York: International Universities Press, 1978, xiii + 669 pp., $35.00.

Lois Murphy Ph.D.

Lois Murphy

Lois B. Murphy, Ph.D.
Washington, D.C.

Lois Murphy  

In relation to the increasing demand for a comprehensive approach to development, this volume offers important strands to the evolving fabric of understanding, without attempting to deal with either biological or ecological differences in mothers, fathers, or children. It provides a stimulating addition to the many studies of specific aspects of child development.

Brody and Axelrad's work is a further development of Brody's long focus on early roots of ego development: her Patterns of Mothering(1956) was an analysis of the mothers' handling of 32 infants from the 128 babies observed by psychologists and a pediatric psychiatrist in Escalona and Leitch's (1952) "non-normative" study in Topeka—a very different town from the urban setting in which Brody and Axelrad's 1970 study of infants and mothers were seen. The present volume presents findings from a followup of 120 of this second group of infants from the ages of two or three to seven years. These babies had come from a large metropolitan hospital and included only those free from defects; their families covered a range from lower- to upper-middle-class socioeconomic groups. The followup involved great effort to locate the families who had moved to other states or even abroad. Since the subjects of our non-normative followups of those Topeka infants still living in Topeka overlapped somewhat with those in Brody's 1956 study, some comparisons will come to mind.

The basic purpose of Brody's studies was to provide empirical evidence for the hypothesis that attitudes and behavior of mothers with their infants show continuity through the early years, and the development of the infant's object relations follows a sequence from need gratification, cathexis of the mother, to things and ideas. Adequacy of the child's adaptation to school at age seven is assumed to be related to the adequacy of the mothering experienced in infancy and subsequently. In the followup study interviews with fathers were added.

The overall organization of the book is designed to first present the data on the mothers with their infants, and the longitudinal data on the children to the age of seven with discussions of continuity of maternal behavior and attitudes, and typology in terms of adequacy of the mothers.

[[RunningHead]]

New Directions in Childhood Psychopathology. Vol. 1: Developmental Considerations

Edited by Saul I. Harrison and John F. McDermott, Jr. New York: International Universities Press, 1981, 600 pp., $35.00.

Donald Cohen M.D.

Donald Cohen

Donald Cohen, M.D.
New York City

Donald Cohen  

This book is designed by the editors to be a companion volume to their earlier (1972) Childhood Psychopathology, and like it, is an anthology. The earlier book, the editors tell us, is a collection of classic readings. This one is an anthology of what are considered to be "the past decade's key publications" (p. xi), devoted to "the major psychopathological categories" (p xii).

It is no surprise, therefore, that many of the 23 papers in this sourcebook have little to do with psychopathology or only deal with issues of illness peripherally. The emphasis is on development "highlighting those biopsychosocial [my italics] variables that have received increasing attention in recent years" (p. xii), and it is an emphasis which is largely uninformed by and unconcerned with analytic data and methodology.

Some of the papers contain data and ideas which truly are new, and some are merely recent restatements in new terms of old material. All, however, are current, and many are in vogue. Psychoanalysis, one might gather from reading this book, is not in vogue—although there are several papers by analysts or using an analytic framework. The thrust of these is developmental (with the exception of Martha Wolfenstein's poignant and excellent paper "How Is Mourning Possible?" wherein she develops the thesis that children are unable to mourn before they have undergone the separation from their parents of adolescence, which constitutes a "trial mourning," where the object remains in reality). Among this group is Margaret Mahler's well-known "Symbiosis and Individuation: The Psychological Growth of the Human Infant"; Selma Fraiberg's "Insights from the Blind: Comparative Studies of Blind and Sighted Infants," which demonstrates the importance of vision in the ego development of the child; Katrina de Hirsch's "Early Language Development," which does the same for language; and the stimulating and provocative paper by Theodore Shapiro and Richard Perry, "Latency Revisited: The Age Seven, Plus or Minus One," which develops the thesis that latency is not ushered in by a biological lessening of the drives, but by the formation, as a result of neurobiological maturation around age seven, of a new and stable cognitive structure which permits drive suppression and the resolution of the Oedipus complex.

[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamics of Psychological Development

By Alexander Thomas and Stella Chess. New York: Brunner/Mazel, 1980, 287 pp., $15.75.

R. Gonzalez M.D.

R. Gonzalez

R. M. Gonzalez, M.D.
Birmingham, Michigan

R. Gonzalez  

The Dynamics of Psychological Development ostensibly is an attempt by Dr. Alexander Thomas and Dr. Stella Chess to express the conclusions derived from their longitudinal studies of normal children and those impaired by congenital rubella. As Dr. Leon Eisenberg states in his Foreword, Thomas and Chess are espousing the idea that psychological development is a "transactional process with the individuality of child, parents, and life experience determining the resultant personality" (p. x).

This book indeed has some chapters that are informative and worth reading: e.g., Chapter V ("The Goals of Behavior: Task Mastery and Social Competence") stresses the process of socialization as a positive and gratifying experience for the child rather than one of frustration and deprivation. Chapter VI ("The Structure of Behavior: Abilities, Motivations and Styles") refers to techniques used by some researchers to study inborn individual traits, with the goal of identifying the children most vulnerable to psychological damage. It also considers the intriguing probability that the polymorphism of the human species is a desirable trait. Chapter VII ("The Process of Development: Interaction and Goodness of Fit") lucidly and convincingly demonstrates the authors' basic postulate, because it shows that the interaction out of which the organism develops is not, as is so often said, between heredity and environment. Rather, it is an interaction between organism and environment. Rather, it is an interaction between organism and environment. Chapter X ("Language and Its Developmental Significance") nicely summarizes recent works on language development. However, this book fails lamentably in a number of important areas: (1) the review of literature is significantly one-sided; (2) it provides little data; (3) the authors' theoretical position is often obscure, and their tenets sometimes hold inner contradictions; and (4) the authors seem to believe that their humanistic approach justifies a summary dismissal of opposing views.

Book Notices

FREUD AND HIS SELF-ANALYSIS. DOWNSTATE TWENTY-FIFTH ANNIVERSARY SERIES, VOL. i. Edited by Mark Kanzer and Jules Glenn. New York: Aronson, 1979, 300 pp., $25.00.

This volume and its companion volume, Freud and His Patients, as well as Volumes III and IV still in preparation, are part of a series offered to commemorate the twenty-fifth anniversary of the Downstate Psychoanalytic Institute in 1974. The authors of the essays presented in this collection were faculty members of the Downstate Institute, which has in the last several years moved its affiliation to the New York University Medical Center and changed its name to The Psychoanalytic Institute at New York University Medical Center. Contributors to this volume, in addition to the editors, are Harold Blum, Roy Lilleskov, Max Schur, Leonard Shengold, and Julian Stamm.

This collection of essays demonstrates what psychoanalytic institutes at their best provide for their members: a rich and nurturing environment that encourages the development of individual creative capacities. That this atmosphere existed at the Downstate Institute is apparent in the high quality of the contributions to this volume. Thirteen of the eighteen papers have appeared previously in other publications, but bringing them together in this volume presents a unique view of Freud's development and thinking, his personal analysis as carried out by himself and others. Kanzer notes in his introduction that Freud's career was built on his disposition to relentlessly dissect his own feelings, adding that "our ability to learn how he did this is based to no small extent on the thousands of letters he wrote in his lifetime, one avenue by which the introspective may reach out to others for a needed sharing of minds." The subject matter of these essays is derived to a great extent from the published Fliess letters, the correspondence with Carl Jung, Karl Abraham, Arnold Zweig, and other letters made available through Ernst Freud.

In "Freud and the Demon" Kanzer states Freud discovered that the irrational was part of normal functioning in himself as well as in others.

[[RunningHead]]

Construction and Reconstruction: Clinical Aspects

Homer Curtis M.D. and Arthur Malin M.D.

Arthur Malin

Homer C. Curtis, M.D.
Arthur Malin, M.D.
9777 Wilshire Boulevard
Beverly Hills, California 90212

Arthur Malin  

HOMER CURTIS OPENED THE PANEL with a brief discussion of the historical and theoretical background of the concept and use of reconstruction. The early roots of reconstruction can be seen in the principle of abreaction, on through the "talking cure" and recovery of childhood memories, by way of shifting emphasis to transference, resistance, and structural considerations in Freud's 1937 paper "Constructions in Analysis." In this paper Freud granted reconstruction equal status with recovery of memories. Freud wrote of the therapeutic need for and value of reconstructions which put together information from memories, dreams, fantasies, transference, affects, and behavior into a more or less coherent picture of the past, which carries conviction for the patient.

Curtis reminded us that Freud went through an "agonizing reappraisal" of his earlier seduction theory which led to the shift in emphasis to the inner life of drives and fantasies. In the case of Dora there is an "environmentalist" tendency, making a general connection between symptoms and earlier life experiences in a direct way without significant attention to endopsychic fantasy elaborations. We see the latter in the case of the Wolf Man and the well-known reconstruction from that case. Curtis noted that there is a periodic emergence of theories of neurosogenesis based on direct-line, molding, or tabularasa-type of environmentalism which ignores the complications of endopsychic transformations, elaborations, and distortions.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association; San Francisco, May, 1980.

Panelists: Joseph D. Lichtenberg, Evelyne Schwaber; Discussants: Samuel Abrams, Arthur Malin, Helen C. Meyers.

Countertransference and Related Subjects

By Harold F. Searles. New York: International Universities Press, 1979, xi + 625 pp., $35.00.

Leon Chattah M.D.

Leon Chattah

Leon Chattah, M.D.
New York City

Leon Chattah  

This book is a compilation of twenty-four of Searles's recent papers. I was impressed and stimulated by the author's observations, less so by his theoretical formulations of the problems, and only rarely by the solutions he proposed. The sedulous study of Searles's imaginative ideas will reward the alert reader with a keener awareness of the meanings and uses of countertransference. The clinical vignettes about his treatment of Mrs. Hendrick and of Mr. Bennett confirmed Searles as a skillful clinician and astute observer of human behavior who is at his best when discussing the inner world of severe borderline and chronic schizophrenic patients.

The main idea in the book is expressed in the chapter on therapeutic symbiosis, "to the extent that the analyst can become able comfortably and freely to immerse himself in the autistic patient as constituting his (the analyst's) world, the patient can then utilize him as a model for identification as regards the acceptance of such very primitive dependency needs, and can come increasingly to exchange his erstwhile autistic world for the world consisting of, and personified by, the analyst" (pp. 189-190). According to Searles, an early level of ego functioning—that of pathologic symbiosis—becomes accessible in the evolution of the transference making it possible for interpretable, therapeutically symbiotic processes to occur. This phase of therapeutic symbiosis ushers in the individuation phase. In "The Patient as Therapist of His Analyst," the interesting observation that transferentially the patient's illness is expressive of his unconscious attempt to cure the doctor is carried too far. Searles states that he knows of "no other determinant of psychological illness that compares, in etiologic importance, with this one" (p. 381). His excellent discussion on the feeling of guilt in the psychoanalyst is flawed by his characteristically provocative, reproachful, and blame-shifting interpretative comments to the patient.

In the chapter on the "dedicated" physician, he concludes that such therapeutic zeal is an expression of the doctor's sadism and of his unconscious wish to maintain the patient's functioning at an immature level. One wonders about Searles's own success in working through this conflict.

[[RunningHead]]

Human Sexuality: A Comparative and Developmental Perspective

Edited by Herant Katchadourian. Berkeley & Los Angeles: University of California Press, 1979, 358 pp., $14.95.

Eleanor Galenson M.D.

Eleanor Galenson

Eleanor Galenson, M.D.
New York City

Eleanor Galenson  

This volume consists of a selection of contributions to a conference held in 1977, the subject of which was a multidisciplinary consideration of human sexual development. The disciplines represented were evolutionary studies, biology, psychology, sociology, and anthropology. In 1974 the National Commission on Population Growth and the American Future initiated a project on Human Sexual Development at Stanford University under the leadership of Herant Katchadourian, the editor of this volume. The conference itself was part of the Special Studies Program of the Stanford Project, and therefore represents an important ongoing scientific venture.

It is a fact that there has been a neglect of research in sexuality as reported in the psychological literature, as the editor states, particularly in comparison to the massive research in such other areas as cognitive development. Those involved in the Stanford project are certainly to be congratulated, therefore, for this review of the studies of sexuality which have been carried out. However, the papers they have chosen for this volume are of uneven quality and on the whole do not contribute a great deal that is new to our knowledge of human sexuality.

This results in part from a surprising neglect of contributions from the psychoanalytic field. The editor alludes to Freud's work in his introductory chapter, stating that Freud's well-known views on infantile sexuality and psychosexual development were based on his study of adults. Yet he disregards Freud's (1905) paper on the development of a phobia in a five-year-old boy, and his other case studies which have served as a basic source of our present-day understanding of infantile sexuality. Among other psychoanalytic contributions omitted were Spitz's (1962) work on autoerotism; Jones's (1927), Horney's (1924), Greenacre's (1950), (1958), Kestenberg's (1956), Fraiberg's (1972), Kleeman's (1971), (1976) and Galenson and Roiphe's (1971), (1976) contributions to the understanding of early female sexual development; Blos's (1962) work on adolescent sexuality; and Greenacre's (1955), (1960), (1969) and Bak's (1968) papers on the sexual perversions. Furthermore, most of the papers in this volume also omit reference to those psychoanalytic contributions. And while it is true that the psychoanalytic studies are not based on systematic observation (save for Kleeman's and Galenson and Roiphe's work), they have nonetheless provided most of our current knowledge of human sexual development.

Eva P. Lester, M.D.
3666 Mc Tavish
Montreal, H3A 1Y2

Eva Lester  

ARE AFFECTS discharge or tension phenomena, or both? Should they rather be conceptualized as ego phenomena, signals for defense action and adaptation? Are they experiences of the self or functions of the ego? Are motives and appetites affective processes, affect derivatives, or parallel to the affect functions of the ego? What is the role of conflict, of cognition, of object relations? Should the psychoanalytic theory of affect concern itself more with content, meaning, and communication than with energy, drives, and functions? These are the questions Peter Hartocollis asked in his introduction to the topic, questions that psychoanalytic theory has failed to answer with any degree of cogency and consistency.

These questions have "tantalized" psychoanalytic theorists since Freud's original equation of affect with instinctual energy, but so far the answers have not amounted to a well-integrated theory of affect. The failure of psychoanalytic theory to come up with good answers in the area of affects stems from the fact that some of the very basic metapsychological concepts, such as instinct, psychic energy, drive cathexis and countercathexis, psychic structure, the ego, the self, are yet to defined comprehensively.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association; San Francisco, May, 1980.

Panelists: Hartvig Dahl, Peter H. Knapp, Otto F. Kernberg.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Platonic Love, Transference Love, and Love in Real Life

Martin Bergmann

Martin Bergmann

Martin S. Bergmann
1136 Fifth Avenue
New York, N.Y. 10028

Martin Bergmann  

SUMMARY

Plato and Freud transformed our way of looking at love. In Plato's Dialogues one can trace the transition and transformation of the mythical view on love into philosophical conceptualizations. The waning of the mythical point of view created the demand for man to know himself, and love became a puzzle. Plato was the first to propose that erotic impulses can undergo sublimation to higher and desexualized aims. Freud was not a Platonist, but if we trace the history of certain ideas it becomes evident that Plato's influence on Freud went further and deeper

109

than was assumed by previous psychoanalytic writers. Freud's conceptualization of the libido can be seen as a Latinized version of Plato's Eros. Some of the difficulties associated with the psychoanalytic use of the term sublimation go back to the Platonic origin of the term. Freud's conviction that tender and aiminhibited love was a later transformation of sexual impulses also went back to Plato, as did his belief that aim-inhibited love endures longer than sexual love. Because erotic love impulses can be sublimated, transference love can be harnessed in the service of cure based on insight. Erotized transference is not based on the refinding of an early love object and therefore is less capable of yielding the therapeutic climate required for psychoanalytic treatment. Freud's treatment procedure confirms Plato's belief in the plasticity of Eros.

[[RunningHead]]

Thoughts on the Emergence of the Sense of Self, with Particular Emphasis on the Body Self

Margaret Mahler M.D. and John McDevitt M.D.

John McDevitt

Margaret S. Mahler, M.D.
10 West 66 Street
New York, New York 10023
John B. McDevitt, M.D.
55 East 87 Street
New York, New York 10028

John McDevitt  

SUMMARY

In trying to trace the emergence of the sense of self during the first fifteen months of life—in particular the formation of the bodily self—we used, on the one hand, severe dissociations of the integral parts of the body self such as occurs in psychosis, in the Gilles de la Tourette syndrome, and in phantom-limb disturbances and, on the other hand, data from our normative study of the separation-individuation process as inferred from mother-infant interaction in general and the infant's behavior in front of the mirror in particular.

The infant's behavior in front of the mirror strongly suggests that feelings about himself only gradually become integrated with perceptual and cognitive awareness of himself. All the data, furthermore, indicate that it is not possible to study the development of the self separate from the development of the object.

All inferences contained in our paper about the emergence of the sense of self were made from the point of view of the central organizing, integrating, and synthesizing institution of the mind, that is, the ego. We agree with other authors that the developing self has both experiential and structural aspects.

We titled our paper "Thoughts on the Emergence of the Sense of Self" to indicate that this paper is meant to be an initial and quite tentative communication about the vast and elusive area of personality development concerning the emergence of our bodily self, and thus of the core of our personal identity.

Paul Dewald M.D.

Paul Dewald

Paul A. Dewald, M.D.
4524 Forest Park Boulevard
Saint Louis, Missouri 63108

Paul Dewald  

SUMMARY

Serious illness in the analyst has a disruptive effect on the therapeutic process. Transference and countertransference responses are highly idiosyncratic and variable, but must be dealt with effectively when the therapeutic process is resumed. Some effects are relatively brief and immediate; others become manifest only after a significant delay. This paper uses the experience of such an illness to call attention to this topic which has been relatively ignored in the psychoanalytic literature.

The Self as Fantasy: Fantasy as Theory

William Grossman M.D.

William Grossman

William I. Grossman, M.D.
45 Oak Avenue
Tenafly, New Jersey 07670

William Grossman  

THE PROBLEM OF THE SELF IN psychoanalysis stands at the intersection of many traditional philosophical and psychoanalytic issues which might be thought of as lying along two axes. The first axis, by far the older, joins the everyday personal experiences of self—that is, self-awareness, self-consciousness, self-observation, self-esteem, self-determination and will—with the ancient philosophical dilemmas concerning a variety of themes, such as mind and body, free will, and the relation between the self and the world of things. The other axis is the psychoanalytic axis with the concrete events of the clinical situation and the subjective experience of the patient at one end, and its mosaic of systematic, theoretical concepts at the other end. The concept of self joins these coordinates and the different perspectives they offer. Although for some psychoanalytic purposes we may not need to keep all of these perspectives in view at any one time, we must not forget the everyday personal and philosophical axis. Even in the realm of theoretical examination, ignoring this axis will covertly narrow and redefine the concept of the self.

Presented at the panel on "Psychoanalytic Theories Of The Self" at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1980.

After this paper was presented, a paper by Spruiell (1981) appeared which both complements and presents alternative ways of looking at the problems presented here.

IN HIS OPENING COMMENTS, Robert Stoller reminded us that the psychoanalytic interest and exploration of male and female sexual development was one of Freud's earliest interests and is a worthy subject for current research. Stoller introduced the panel topic by defining several terms in current use; sex was defined as the biological and physiological basis for male and female bodily development; gender, previously synonymous with sex, has taken on the meaning of degrees of masculinity and femininity.

Until recently, our knowledge was restricted to the understanding of sexual development. But in the past twenty years a change has occurred; data from analysis, biology, infant observation, and family studies provided exciting new information. There has been increasing focus on issues of perinatal, preoedipal, and dyadic development; bonding; prelanguage cognition; early fusion-merging with the object and its resolution in the separation-individuation phase. This excites hope and enthusiasm for a new scientific methodology for psychoanalysis. It also implies less dependence on transference material from adult analysis, as rich and valuable as that may be. These new methodologies allow for scientific rigor as well as discourse with other disciplines.

Held at the Annual Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association; San Francisco, May 1980.

Panelists: Eleanor Galenson, Henri Parens, Phyllis Tyson; Discussant: Robert Westfall.

The Chairman of the panel, Robert S. Wallerstein, introduced the formal presentations with a historical overview. He noted that in 1926 Freud, under the title The Question of Lay Analysis, wrote his spirited defense of Theodor Reik, a prominent nonmedical member of the Vienna Psychoanalytic Society, who was charged with a breach of an old Austrian law against "quackery"—a law which made it illegal for a person without a medical degree to treat patients.

The response to Freud's treatise among psychoanalysts was immediate, and it brought to the fore wide-ranging differences of opinion. A long series of 26 reasoned responses by prominent analysts from seven leading centers on the two continents was published simultaneously in the following year in the two official psychoanalytic journals of the time.

Held at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York City, December 19, 1980.

Panelists: Homer C. Curtis, Robert Michels, Albert J. Solnit, Herbert J. Schlesinger.

Serious Illness in the Analyst: Countertransference Considerations

Sander Abend M.D.

Sander Abend

Sander M. Abend, M.D.
1040 Park Avenue
New York, New York 10028

Sander Abend  

SOME YEARS AGO I had an illness that necessitated interruption of my practice for a number of weeks. There was a substantial interval between the establishment of the diagnosis and the actual institution of treatment, during which I was able to continue working, so there was ample opportunity for me to consult with colleagues about how best to handle the situation with my patients. As Dewald (1982) points out, the literature offers next to nothing of use on the subject, a fact which in itself requires explanation. I made notes on my patients' reactions to what I told them in anticipation of the break in our work together, and then again after its resumption. However, without much critical self-examination, I subsequently decided that what I had observed was not worth reporting. When the opportunity arose to discuss Dewald's excellent and courageous study, which focuses on his own as well as his patients' reactions, I was stimulated to reconsider my material and the reasons for having put it aside.

It is my intention to raise certain questions about the technical management of these situations when they arise, and about the role of countertransference reactions in influencing technical decisions.

Presented at the Fall Meeting of the American Psychoanalytic Association, New York, December, 1980.

"Developmental Lag" in the Evolution of Technique for Psychoanalysis of Neurotic Conflict

Paul Gray M.D.

Paul Gray

Paul Gray, M.D.
3315 Wisconsin Avenue, N.W.
Washington, D.C. 20016

Paul Gray  

SUMMARY

The practice of psychoanalysis of neurotic conflict has not—to the extent that might be expected—evolved a consistent use in technique of available knowledge of the ego's mechanisms of defense. This is primarily related to a general resistance that can usefully be regarded as a "developmental lag." The evidence for this, particularly in Freud's writings, is reviewed as are the observations of others who have made note of this phenomenon. Some of the reasons that have contributed to this resistance among analysts are discussed.

SUMMARY

I have explored the concepts of self, ego, affects, and drives, with special emphasis on terminological confusions and Freud's use of Selbst and Ich. I have also proposed a modification of the dual instinct theory in the light of the relation between affect and drives. Finally, I have proposed a developmental model on the basis of all the above considerations, and stressed its relevance for the classification of nonorganic psychopathology.

Merton Gill M.D. and Irwin Hoffman Ph.D.

Irwin Hoffman

Merton M. Gill, M.D.
Irwin Z. Hoffman, Ph.D.
University of Illinois at the Medical Center
P. O. Box 6998
Chicago, Illinois 60680

Irwin Hoffman  

SUMMARY

We have described a coding scheme for obtaining an objective account of the analysis of resisted aspects of the patient's experience of the relationship with the therapist in single, audio-recorded sessions of psychoanalysis and psychotherapy. The focus is on what is often regarded as an important first step in the analysis of transference: the identification and exploration of the preconscious points of attachment of transference ideas in the here-and-now. Related to the scheme is the view that there is generally a plausible basis in the here-and-now for the patient's ideas about the therapist. The implications of that view for the concept of transference are discussed. Future directions for research are noted, including improving the reliability of the scheme and testing various hypotheses regarding how the particular step in technique assessed by the method affects other aspects of the process and outcome.

In discussion of these steps an effort was made to distinguish between core morphological or core gender identity—that is, the primary sense of being male or female, to which other aspects of gender identity are added over the course of development to eventually include a "mix" of masculinity and femininity; gender role identity, which includes the conscious and unconscious mental representations of dialogues with other people vis-à-vis one's gender identity as well as identification with role models; and the separate process of choosing a sexual partner orientation. [[RunningHead]]

The Choice of a Name: "Dora" and Freud's Relationship with Breuer

Hannah Decker Ph.D.

Hannah Decker

Hannah S. Decker, Ph.D.
Department of History University of Houston Central Campus
Houston, Texas 77004

Hannah Decker  

SUMMARY

I have suggested that Freud's choice of the pseudonym Dora for his eighteen-year-old hysterical patient, Ida Bauer, was over-determined. Dora, it seems likely, was named not only after Freud's sister's nursemaid, as Freud himself explained, but also after Dora Breuer, Josef Breuer's youngest daughter. This theory is based on an examination of the similarities in the lives and symptoms of Anna O. (Breuer's famous hysterical patient of 1880–1882) and Dora; on an analysis of the transferences and countertransferences in the cases of these two young women; and on evidences of the persistent significance of Josef Breuer in Freud's life after 1895. These specific inquiries also call attention to the nature of hysteria at the end of the nineteenth century and to the ever-present complexities of the physicianpatient relationship.

[[RunningHead]]

The Nature of the Oedipus Complex in the Dora Case

Alan Krohn Ph.D. and Janis Krohn M.S.

Janis Krohn

Alan Krohn, Ph.D.
Janis Krohn, M.S.W.
470 Rock Creek Drive
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

Janis Krohn  

SUMMARY

In this paper we sought to study, through a detailed review of Dora's analytic material, the specific nature of her oedipal conflict and its expression in her treatment with Freud as manifested in her dreams, associations, and transference. We found that while Freud was surely correct in considering Dora's positive oedipal conflict as an important source of her psycho-pathology, the implication of her early regression to a phallic-oedipal position was not fully appreciated or interpreted, leading to her eventual premature termination. While Freud analyzed, largely following the end of the treatment, Dora's erotic love for Frau K., he did not see this love as deriving from an integrated phallic-oedipal complex which included competitive and castrative strivings toward men as well as wishes to love a woman from a phallic position. Thus we sought to extend Freud's analysis of the case by delineating the specific form of Dora's homosexual love, the genetic sources for it, and the aggressive wish toward men as they derive from a particular substage of the oedipal phase for females, the phallic-oedipal phase. We conclude that Dora's hostility toward Freud, her father and Herr. K. resulted not only from injured pride but, more basically, from her jealousy and rivalry with father for mother's love. In her treatment Dora's phallic-oedipal wishes became increasingly dominant. They were manifested in the transference with her first acting to engage Freud's interest and then thwarting his interpretive efforts, ultimately by leaving the treatment altogether. The case well illustrates the importance of understanding the role of the phallic-oedipal phase in female hysterical patients.

[[RunningHead]]

The Exit Line: Heightened Transference-Countertransference Manifestations at the End of the Hour

Glen Gabbard M.D.

Glen Gabbard

Glen O. Gabbard, M.D.
The Menninger Foundation
P.O. Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Glen Gabbard  

SUMMARY

Final comments in many contexts have privileged positions among the many communications we make to one another as human beings. Deathbed pronouncements are treasured for their alleged profundities. "Famous last words" are catalogued. As we listen to political debates, we are particularly attentive to the closing statements of the candidates in the belief that something of superordinate importance will be revealed in these final words. Patients who come to us for psychotherapy and psychoanalysis also convey special messages in their parting comments. Final words are heavily invested because they bear the feelings deriving from earlier separations, complete with the longings to fuse with the earliest objects. Moreover, the cathexis belonging to the object may indeed be displaced onto words. In this paper I have sought to delineate and to describe certain patterns of exit lines which may provide useful information for the clinician. These "last words" are saved for the hour's end to keep them out of the therapeutic process and to render the therapist impotent and unable to respond. However, the therapist need not despair because he can bring these comments into the following hour as the focus of much productive work. After all, as Scarlet O'Hara would say, "Tomorrow is another day."

[[RunningHead]]

On Some Clinical Implications of Action Language

Donald Spence Ph.D.

Donald Spence

Donald P. Spence, Ph.D.
Department of Psychiatry Rutgers Medical School
CMDN
Piscataway, New Jersey 08854

Donald Spence  

SUMMARY

Schafer's action language can be criticized on purely theoretical grounds; here, the emphasis is on some of its clinical implications. First, action language tends to put the patient into an adversary position with respect to the analyst; this interferes with the therapeutic alliance, discourages regression, and encourages self-consciousness and problem-solving reasoning. Second, action language tempts the analyst to use premature labels in place of ambiguous metaphors; premature clarification may undercut the process of analytic discovery. Third, a focus on action and responsibility, because it highlights conscious mechanisms and the secondary process, may run the danger of working against the analytic process. It may yield rationalization rather than insight and convey the message that the analyst does not take seriously the concept of the unconscious. Fourth, by emphasizing clarity in favor of metaphor, action language may put

183

an unnecessary emphasis on rationality and may discourage the kind of groping self-exploration that is the essence of productive analysis.

Notes on Critical Examinations of the Primal Scene Concept

Gerhard Dahl Ph.D.

Gerhard Dahl

Gerhard Dahl, Ph.D.
Kaiserdamm 85
D-1000 Berlin 19, West Germany

Gerhard Dahl  

IN THE RECENT GERMAN LITERATURE there has been no paper devoted especially to the primal scene. Where the topic is mentioned, it is not given much importance, and there is agreement with Freud's original writing. This is particularly noteworthy in view of the fact that in the United States many treatises on the primal scene have been published during the last ten years, indicating an obvious interest in this topic, and there is no lack of critical discussions of Freud's statement.

Why would a subject to which Freud attributed substantial importance be treated and judged so differently? One would assume that the primal scene is equally ubiquitous in both countries. Parents are advised by "progressive" educators to let children take part in their sex life in the hope this will prevent sexual disturbances later on. This and similar advice cannot be simply discarded as extreme. In Germany this advice has been applied predominantly by fringe groups only. Yet in the United States this practice is of longer standing, and conceivably exerts a stronger influence on the sociocultural atmosphere than in Germany. It seems more difficult to extricate oneself from such an atmosphere, which conveys the so-called Zeitgeist, than from a delineated, palpable ideology. An adaptation to it often occurs slowly and without special awareness.

SUMMARY

This paper explores the meanings and functions of a transference paradigm of a fantasied twin relationship with the analyst. Twin transference in the analysis of nontwins has been reported infrequently in the psychoanalytic literature, except recently by Kohut and his co-workers, who refer to twinship transference as a variant of narcissistic mirror transference. We suggest that such narrow definition tends to reduce the complexity of the wish for a twinlike relationship with the analyst. Analytic data are used to show the advantages of examining twin transference within structural theory, in terms of the multiple functions served by this rather primitive transference paradigm, rather than reducing it only to one variant of the need for certain mirroring functions. We suggest that twin transference, together with all twin fantasies, subserves multiple functions, of which we highlight gratification and defense against the dangers of intense object need. In this formulation, the twinlike representation of the object provides the illusion of influence or control over the object by the pretense of being able to impersonate or transform oneself into the object and the object into the self. Intense object need persists together with a partial narcissistic defense against full acknowledgment of the object by representing the sought-after object as combining aspects of self and other. Further analytic attention needs to be directed to the specific representation of the needed object in certain primitive transference paradigms instead of exclusive emphasis on the functions required of the object. Our analytic data are used to argue that intense early needs of an object are best understood analytically within a conflict model in which they are modified by multiple wishes, drives, fears, dangers, and needs for defense.

Leo Rangell M.D.

Leo Rangell

Leo Rangell, M.D.
456 North Carmelina Avenue
Los Angeles, California 90049

Leo Rangell  

ALTERNATIVE PSYCHOLOGICAL THEORIES of the self have typically brought with them accompanying changes in psychoanalytic theory and practice more significant than the suggested change in the role of the self. These changes, as in the most currently discussed of such new theories, which takes for itself the title "the psychology of the self" (Kohut, 1971), (1977), offer not new paradigms within psychoanalysis, but alternative psychoanalytic theories of mental functioning. What is left, for example, of psychoanalysis when an analyst judges a patient to be an instance of "Tragic Man," by far more common in this view than "Guilty Man," resulting from a psychic deficit brought about by parental failure from the beginning? The psychoanalytic aim which follows and the nature of the psychoanalytic process to achieve it bear no resemblance to psychoanalytic understanding or treatment as we know it. There are no longer unconscious conflicts to be understood and an analytic stance to reach them, but a corrective emotional experience—not called that—to bring the patient up again as he was not brought up before.

[[RunningHead]]

Fixing Women: Devaluation, Idealization, and the Female Fetish

Elizabeth Waites Ph.D.

Elizabeth Waites

Elizabeth A. Waites, Ph.D.
206 South Main Street, Suite 200
Ann Arbor, Michigan 48104

Elizabeth Waites  

SUMMARY

A particular ideal of femininity which has long been popular in our culture is the image of the esthetically perfect but behaviorally

457

compliant woman. By comparing two clinical cases, I have attempted to show that this stylized image is sometimes part of a specific defensive structure, enacted in extreme forms as part of a perverse ritual. The idealized image is a secondary formation with roots in early transitional representations. As an adult defense, it isolates devalued attributes, particularly anal attributes, from an esthetic ideal and enables the narcissistically disturbed individual to effect an illusion of separation while maintaining compulsive control over a needed object.

The enactment of this defensive pattern frequently requires the cooperation of a partner. Women comply not only for masochistic reasons or because of intimidation, but because the fetishistic ritual itself is often an important part of their defensive armory. Women who rely on the idealization provided by a partner as the major source of their self-esteem are sometimes willing to accept pathological features of a partnership rather than give up this narcissistic support.

At the more creative end of the continuum between pathological and adaptive transitional representations, the idealized image of woman may lend a playful romantic tone to relationships between men and women. In its most compulsive manifestations, however, it tends to perpetuate superficiality and impedes understanding between the sexes. Patients in whom the ideal is elaborated in its most distorted forms offer a useful opportunity to explore the roots of these phenomena.

Arnold Richards

Ernst A. Ticho, Ph.D.
Arnold D. Richards, M.D.
40 East 89th Street
New York, New York 10028

Arnold Richards  

Ernst A. Ticho presented the opening paper, "The Alternate Schools and the Self." In accord with his belief that the study of "alternate" schools of psychoanalysis is relevant to the evaluation of new psychoanalytic therapeutic approaches, Ticho discussed the approaches to the self of the schools of Adler, Jung, Horney, and Sullivan. Although their definitions of self vary considerably, these schools all emphasize the importance of the subjective, living, creative, experiencing, spontaneous aspects of the psyche in their theories, and criticize what they consider Freud's fragmenting and mechanistic approach to the self. They stress the unity of the self, its indivisibility (Adler) and individuation (Jung). In general, these theories share an optimistic and inspirational orientation with strong moralistic overtones. As a result of stressing the creative self and the meaning and purpose of life, Adler, Jung, and Horney all opposed theories (like Freud's) that stressed inner conflict. Nonsexual meanings were emphasized at the expense of sexual meanings, and the dynamic unconscious was either downplayed, forgotten (Adler), or subordinated to the collective unconscious (Jung). For the alternate schools, synthesis proved more important than analysis. Freud, on the other hand, did not find it necessary to undertake a special synthesis after analysis; for him the unity of the self was, so to speak, self-understood. It should be noted, of course, that Freud's perspective antedates the "widening scope of psychoanalysis" and recent work with more regressed patients.

[[RunningHead]]

The Theory of Gender Identity Disorders

Jon Meyer M.D.

Jon Meyer

Jon K. Meyer, M.D.
The Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine
Phipps 009
600 North Wolfe Street
Baltimore, Maryland 21205

Jon Meyer  

SUMMARY

Experience with more than 500 patients over the last decade has led to the conclusion that the quest for sex reassignment is a symptomatic compromise formation serving defensive and expressive functions. The symptoms are the outgrowth of developmental trauma affecting body ego and archaic sense of self and caused by peculiar symbiotic and separation-individuation phase relationships. The child exists in the pathogenic (and reparative) maternal fantasy in order to repair her body image and to demonstrate the interconvertability of the sexes.

Gender identity exists not as a primary phenomenon, but in a sense as a tertiary one. There is, no doubt, a tendency to gender-differentiate in a way concordant with biological endowment. Nevertheless, gender formation is seriously compromised by earlier psychological difficulty. Gender identity is a fundamental acquisition in the developing personality, but it is part of a hierarchical series beginning with archaic body ego, early body image, and primitive selfness, representing their extension into sexual and reproductive spheres.

Gender identity consolidates during separation-individuation and gender pathology bears common features with other preoedipal syndromes. Transsexualism is closely linked to perversions, and the clinical syndromes may shade from one into another. However, what is kept at the symbolic level in the perversions must be made concrete in transsexualism. In this regard there is a close relation to psychosis.

The clinical complaint of the transsexual is a condensation of remarkable proportions. When the transsexual says that he is a girl trapped in a man's body, he sincerely means what he says. As with other symptoms, however, it takes a long time before he begins to say what he means.

Judith Chused

Judith F. Chused, M.D.
1805 Randolph Street, Northwest
Washington, D.C. 20011

Judith Chused  

SUMMARY

The analyses of two children and one adolescent were presented to illustrate the concept that the neutrality of the analyst can be used not only to (a) establish a working, analyzing, and observing alliance, (b) permit the development, recognition, and working through of the transference neurosis, but also to (c) develop a sense of autonomy and self—esteem which had been contaminated by the neediness and lack of true empathy of the primary objects during the practicing and rapprochement phases of separation–individuation. For the patients discussed above, many ego functions which should have had a degree of secondary autonomy were either inhibited, enmeshed in conflict, or experienced as nongenuine, part of a "false self." It was as if the experience with the neutral analyst permitted an "autonomous practicing" that had not been possible during the period of separation—individuation.

The Resolution of a Transsexual Wish in a Five-Year-Old Boy

Loretta Loeb M.D. and Morton Shane M.D.

Morton Shane

Loretta Loeb, M.D.
Morton Shane, M.D.
1908 S.W. Terrace Drive
Portland, Oregon 9720

Morton Shane  

SUMMARY

The brief truncated psychoanalytic treatment of a transsexual boy described in this paper demonstrates the development of transference reactions and their subsequent resolution. The analytic situation allowed this young patient to live out again his relationship with his parents in his relationship with the analyst, to recover from wishing to be a member of the female sex, and to work through his conflicted symbiosis with his mother. Carlos became able to individuate and disidentify from her into a separate male child, in order to be able to identify with, and accept, his own biological and psychological maleness. It is to be hoped that our success with this patient will encourage others to treat and study children suffering from gender identity problems via the psychoanalytic approach.

[[RunningHead]]

The Superordinate Self in Psychoanalytic Theory and in the Self Psychologies

Arnold Richards M.D.

Arnold Richards

Arnold D. Richards, M.D.
40 East 89 Street
New York, New York 10028

Arnold Richards  

SUMMARY

The concept of the self has been used in several attempts to resolve the epistemological problems of what is subjective and what is objective, what is personal and what is organismic. In addition, it has been used to mediate between the hermeneutic and natural-science approaches to psychoanalytic explanation, between the motivational and causal dimensions of our theory and experience. In the case of Kohut, the self was initially invoked to deal with clinical difficulties associated with the analysis of patients with narcissistic personality disorders; more recently, it has become the central article in a "self psychology" that addresses presumed deficiencies in the traditional psychoanalytic picture of psychopathology. But the concept of the self is not suited to be a panacea for resolving theoretical or clinical difficulties. The self as person refers to an entity that is both enduring and changing; it describes continuity in the face of change and change in the face of continuity. Abend (1974) comes closest to capturing this attribute of the self in his image of the tidal beach with a configuration that changes but an essence that remains the same. Eisnitz (1980) evokes something similar in his figure-ground conception of the self-representation. The crux of the matter is that the notion of self-experience includes a variety of phenomena that cannot be contained

955

within a single self-construct—be it normal pathologic, grandiose, or otherwise.

As a result of these considerations, I have argued against the use of the self as a superordinate concept in psychoanalytic theory and have focused on the shortcomings of three self psychologies that use the self in this way. I believe that Klein, Gedo, and Kohut all offer the self as a kind of conceptual tranquilizer for the philosophical, theoretical, and clinical dualities that are inherent in psychoanalytic work. Grossman addressed himself to these dualities as far back as 1967 and elaborated on the problem with Simon (1969) in a pathbreaking paper on anthropomorphism in psychoanalysis. Grossman and Simon contended that the controversy about anthropomorphism in psychoanalytic theory pertains to the basic confusion in psychology between meaning and causality. They submitted that until this confusion was dispelled and until some superordinate concept was found that could "encompass both kinds of discourse," attempts to transform psychoanalysis into a general psychology would result in failure. In my view, the superordinate schema invoked by Grossman and Simon in 1969 remains to be formulated, the self psychologies of today notwithstanding.

THE OPULENT PROFUSION OF ANALYTIC THOUGHT and scholarship in this group of papers on "Theories of the Self and Psychoanalytic Concepts" stimulated reflection and commentary/about the subject itself, the relevance of the topic to contemporary psychoanalytic controversy, and some lines of development and areas of confusion concerning definition, boundaries, and relations between theories of the self and psychoanalytic theory.

Whatever the self is is not self-evident. The concepts vary tremendously; they involve different frames of reference and different levels of discourse. They range from the colloquial mode of speech when we talk phenomenologically, e.g., about self-awareness or self-criticism, all the way to highly abstract metapsychological constructions. They range from academic psychology to psychoanalysis, and as surveyed by Ticho (1982), to different schools of thought surrounding psychoanalysis. They go back to controversies that have been with us for a very long time.

Los Angeles, California 90024

Gilbert Herdt  

SUMMARY

We have examined, using data from a Stone-Age New Guinea culture, two hypotheses regarding the development of masculinity: that a prolonged and too gratifying mother-son symbiosis threatens a boy's chances of becoming masculine and that a boy, to create masculinity, must raise a psychic barrier against the urge to be merged with his mother. The data show the hypothesized forces to be at work in these New Guineans much as in Western society.
