Freud: The Man and the Cause

By Ronald W. Clark. New York: Random House, 1980, xii + 632 pp., $19.95.

Arnold Rogow Ph.D.

Arnold Rogow

Arnold A. Rogow, Ph.D.
New York City

Arnold Rogow  

Any biography of Freud, even one that is shorter and more compact, invites comparison with Jones's (1953-1957)The Life and Work of Sigmund Freud, which constitutes, in effect, Freud's official biography. Jones, who knew Freud and his family for many years—although not as many years as he claimed—apparently enjoyed the full cooperation of Anna Freud (The Life and Work is dedicated to her), and he had other advantages over writers on Freud before him and since. Jones, whose analytic career extended back more than a half-century, unlike most biographers of Freud, could write knowledgeably and perceptively about psychoanalysis itself, and he also was friendly or at least acquainted with almost everyone who played an important part in the psychoanalytic movement during its formative years.

But these advantages over Ronald W. Clark, Freud's latest biographer, while they give substantial support to Jones's self-proclaimed role of authoritative "insider," do not work entirely in his favor. Biographers who are close to their subjects, like journalists who are intimate with those in power, or, nearer home, analysts who are friendly with their patients, compound the transference problems in such relationships. Jones was not without such problems. Whether or not Jones is guilty of excessive partisanship in his accounts of the personality clashes and doctrinal conflicts that plagued psychoanalysis in the early period, as some have alleged, he is vulnerable to the charge that he occasionally makes too much of his own relationship with Freud, and too little of Freud's propensity to behave, at times, much like other men behave when they confront the uncertainties and problems of life.

Thus Jones writes, in his preface to the first volume, that he had been "in constant intimate contact with Freud … [and] a close friend for forty years …" (p. xiii). In fact, as he mentions elsewhere, he did not meet Freud until April 26, 1908, on which occasion he was introduced to Freud by Jung; and he did not attend a meeting of the "Psychological Wednesday Society," the predecessor of the Vienna Psycho-Analytical Society, until May 6, 1908, when he appeared there as a "guest" (Vol. 2, pp. 42-43). Two years later Freud still was addressing Jones in letters as "Dear Dr. Jones," instead of the more familiar salutation of "Dear Jones," with which he later headed his letters to Jones (Vol. 2, pp. 62-66, 202ff.). So far as I can determine, Jones was never "Dear Friend" or "Dear Colleague," as were Ferenczi and Abraham, much less "Dear Son," the greeting Freud twice used in letters to Ferenczi (Vol. 2, pp. 164–167).

Psychoanalysis and the History of the Individual

By Hans Loewald. New Haven, Conn.: Yale Univ. Press, 1978, x + 77 pp., $7.95.

Stuart Asch M.D.

Stuart Asch

Stuart Asch, M.D.
New York City

Stuart Asch  

Loewald's Freud lectures at Yale in 1978, which comprise this book, were presented to an interdisciplinary audience as "tentative philosophical reflections on psychoanalysis." The three essays present his views on man as a moral agent, transference and love, and religious experience. He hopes to demonstrate how psychoanalysis, "a rather untidy discipline," can explicate and raise valuable questions about these areas of psychological functioning.

To fully appreciate the richness of Loewald's contribution, the analyst needs to fill out the background of these lectures which were designed for a sophisticated but lay audience. Since familiarity with his previous, more technical, writings will be helpful, I shall refer to them as I progress.

To a large extent this is a philosophical treatise. As with Freud's death instinct (with which Loewald [1972] agrees with some modification, and which, like Freud, he uses to partially explain the negative therapeutic reaction), its relation to clinical observation is not immediate and should not be judged on the basis of clinical observation. The concepts he discusses are akin to that level of Waelder's (1962) hierarchy of psychoanalytic knowledge which is furthest removed from clinical data. It is important to recognize,as with Freud's philosophical ideas, fine clinical work can be done without accepting them.

In his first lecture, "Man as a Moral Agent," Loewald requires the healthy person to be able to maintain some access to his dynamic unconscious and infantile self (including its affects, its experiences in relating to early objects, etc.). This to-and-fro movement within the psychic apparatus is considered not only enriching, but necessary for unrestricted functioning; each individual must learn about his past and accept it as a part of his current self-representation.

Loewald insists it is a moral requirement that the individual integrate his id into his conscious life. The theme of morality being the integration and awareness of previously unconnected and unaware internal milieus resonates with one of Schafer's (1976) aims in "action language"—to help the individual recognize and accept his own active role in his psychology and relations with the world.

Eleanor Galenson

Eleanor Galenson, M.D.
New York City

Eleanor Galenson  

Research concerning psychological development during infancy has proceeded so rapidly during the past decade that this report of the Harvard-connected Day Care Project, begun in 1968 and discontinued several years later, might well have been outdated by now. To the contrary, however, some of the findings of this project provoke a rethinking of current views of early development, particularly since they were unexpected in part. Furthermore, they tend to support certain psychoanalytically based theoretical ideas, in spite of the fact that the authors tend to view development primarily from a cognitive standpoint.

The study was planned to clarify two major issues: first, can one predict from the psychological profile of a one-year-old the more complex psychological profile several years later; second, what effect does regular day care center attendance have on the development of infants compared to those infants reared entirely at home. Both issues are extremely complex ones, and the variables which have to be considered are discussed at great length and most lucidly by Kagan in the chapters that constitute almost the first two thirds of the book. (Incidentally, an excellent review of pertinent literature is included in this portion.)

One example of the complexity of the research issues is the question of what actually constitutes psychological development; this is determined at least in part by the cultural and professional biases of the particular researchers, as Kagan indicates. In this project, one index of psychological development was the degree of separation anxiety and when it appeared. Another index was the infant's degree of attentiveness, vocalization, and smiling in response to visual and auditory stimuli or events. Analysis of the research data appeared to confirm Kagan's (1971) earlier research findings that there are major discontinuities in these particular dimensions of psychological development.

The research design consisted of a group of 33 infants, either first- or second-born, the products of full-term normal pregnancies, without serious physical disorders, and between three and one half and five and one half months of age at time of entry. No other selection criteria were applied, each child being admitted in turn as applications were received from parents who lived in the community and learned of the project through their neighbors.

Beyond Interpretation

By John Gedo. New York: International Universities Press, 1978, xii + 280 pp., $22.50.

Robert Michels M.D.

Robert Michels

Robert Michels, M.D.
New York City

Robert Michels  

John Gedo's Beyond Interpretation aims at a "revised theory for psychoanalysis." It is built upon the foundation of Gedo and Goldberg's Models of the Mind, and presents the extension of Gedo's (although apparently not Goldberg's) thinking since the earlier work. Its goal is ambitious: "to demontrate that our current psychological insights, properly applied … give us unprecedented opportunities to assist our analysands to alter their subsequent functioning." Gedo's concept of psychoanalysis is that interpretation is only one of five coequal therapeutic methods, each appropriate for the problems characteristic of a specific mode of psychological functioning. The characteristic problems and appropriate therapies are overstimulation and pacification, disruption of self-cohesion and unification, persistence of illusion and optimal disillusionment, conflict and interpretation, and finally frustration and introspection. At one point, he even suggests the possibility of a sixth mode, beyond frustration, characterized by creation and poetry, but this is only a hint.

Gedo's psychoanalysis encompasses all of these therapies. Each will be necessary in every psychoanalysis, since each patient presents problems in all modes, and the treatment of issues related to later modes requires the prior resolution of issues stemming from earlier modes. However, in one passage, Gedo defines psychoanalysis as "the process of acquiring self-knowledge through reliving crucial childhood transactions within the treatment setting," suggesting that insight is at least primum inter pares.

Gedo's interest in the more primitive modes of psychological functioning, the patients in whom they are prominent (a large group of those he considers analyzable), and the therapeutic strategies that they elicit direct his attention to the crucial role of the first few years of life in psychic development. He argues that "only by altering the outcome of each of the earlier developmental vicissitudes" (i.e., by pacification, unification, etc.), "does a more favorable resolution of later conflicts become possible" and criticizes those who believe "preoedipal material in analysis can be interpreted simply as regressive evasion of the Oedipus complex." The critical issue of course is the phrase "in analysis." There is no doubt that there are individuals for whom the major themes of psychopathology, and of life, stem from "earlier developmental vicissitudes," themes that are prominent in their own right, not as regressive evasions of later conflicts.
